en: Gouqi (Lycium / wolfberry). Taste bitter, cold nature. Indicated for evil qi within the five viscera, heat in the middle with wasting-thirst, and bi-syndrome (painful obstruction) throughout the body. Taken long-term, strengthens sinews and bones, lightens the body, and delays aging. Also called Qigen, Digu, Goujii, Difu. Grows in marshy lowlands.

Juyou (tangerine/pomelo peel). Taste pungent, warm. Indicated for concretions and heat with rebellious qi in the chest; promotes the free flow of water and grain (digestion). Taken long-term, removes bad breath, lowers qi, and clears the spirit. Also called Jupi (tangerine peel). Grows in the mountain valleys of the south.

Ganqi (dried lacquer). Taste pungent, warm; non-toxic. Indicated for severe injury; supplements the middle, mends sinews and bones, fills the marrow and brain, calms the five viscera, and treats the five slacknesses and six urgencies, and wind-cold-damp bi. Fresh lacquer kills long worms (intestinal parasites). Taken long-term, lightens the body and delays aging. Grows in river valleys.

Mulan (magnolia). Taste bitter, cold. Indicated for severe heat within the skin; removes facial heat, redness, pustules and rosacea, aversion to wind, leprosy-like sores, and itching dampness beneath the genitals; brightens the eyes. Also called Linlan. Grows in mountain valleys.

Nüzhenshi (glossy privet fruit, Ligustrum lucidum). Taste bitter, neutral. Mainly supplements the middle, calms the five viscera, nourishes essence and spirit, and removes the hundred diseases. Taken long-term, makes one plump and healthy, lightens the body, and prevents aging. Grows in river valleys.

Sangshang jisheng (mulberry mistletoe). Taste bitter, neutral. Indicated for lower back pain, stiff back in children, welling-abscesses and swelling; secures the fetus, fills out the flesh, strengthens teeth and hair, and lengthens the beard and eyebrows. Its fruit brightens the eyes, lightens the body, and clears the spirit. Also called Jixie, Yumu, Wantong. Grows in mountain valleys.

Ruihe (蕤核, Prinsepia seed). Taste sweet, neutral; non-toxic. Indicated for knotted evil qi in the heart and abdomen; brightens the eyes, and treats red, painful eyes with tearing. Taken long-term, lightens the body, boosts qi, and staves off hunger. Grows in river valleys.

Longyan (longan). Taste sweet, neutral. Indicated for evil qi in the five viscera; calms the will and relieves loss of appetite. Taken long-term, strengthens the ethereal soul (hun), sharpens the mind, lightens the body, prevents aging, and clears the spirit. Grows in mountain valleys.

Houpo (magnolia bark). Taste bitter, warm; non-toxic. Indicated for wind-stroke, cold damage (shanghan), headache, cold-heat with fright qi, and blood bi with numb, dead flesh; expels the three worms. Grows in mountain valleys.

Zhuye (bamboo leaf). Taste bitter, neutral. Indicated for cough with rebellious ascending qi, overflowing sinew spasms, and malign sores; kills small worms. The root, made into a decoction, boosts qi, quenches thirst, supplements deficiency, and lowers qi. Its juice treats wind convulsions and bi. The fruit clears the spirit, lightens the body, and boosts qi.

Zhishi (bitter orange, immature fruit). Taste bitter, cold. Indicated for severe wind in the skin causing itching like hemp seeds or beans; removes cold-heat and heat binding, and stops dysentery. Builds up flesh, benefits the five viscera, boosts qi, and lightens the body. Grows in river marshes.

Shanzhuyu (Cornus / Asiatic cornelian cherry). Taste sour; non-toxic. Indicated for evil qi below the heart with cold-heat; warms the middle, expels cold-damp bi, and removes the three worms. Taken long-term, lightens the body. Also called Shuzao. Grows in mountain valleys.

Wuzhuyu (evodia fruit). Taste pungent, warm. Mainly warms the middle, lowers qi, and stops pain; treats cough with rebellious qi and cold-heat, removes damp blood bi, expels wind evil, and opens the interstices (couli). The root kills the three worms. Also called Yi. Grows in river valleys.

Qinpi (ash bark). Taste sour; non-toxic. Indicated for wind-cold-damp bi and shivering chills; removes heat, and treats bluish nebula and white film in the eyes. Taken long-term, keeps the hair from turning white and lightens the body. Grows in river valleys.

Zhizi (gardenia fruit). Taste bitter, cold. Indicated for evil qi within the five viscera, heat qi in the stomach, facial redness, wine-induced pustules and rosacea, red nose, and white or red leprosy-type sores and ulcers. Also called Mudan. Grows in river valleys.

Hehuan (silk tree, Albizia). Taste sweet, neutral. Mainly calms the five viscera, harmonizes the heart and will, and makes one joyful and free of worry. Taken long-term, lightens the body, brightens the eyes, and grants one's desires. Grows in the mountain valleys of Yizhou.

Qinjiao (Sichuan pepper). Taste pungent, warm. Indicated for wind evil qi; warms the middle, removes cold bi, strengthens the teeth, lengthens the hair, and brightens the eyes. Taken long-term, lightens the body, improves the complexion, delays aging, extends the years, and clears the spirit. Grows in river valleys.

Ziwei (trumpet creeper / Campsis flower). Taste sour, slightly cold. Indicated for women's post-nursing ailments, flooding (profuse uterine bleeding), concretions and blood blockage, and cold-heat with emaciation; nourishes the fetus. Grows in river valleys.

Wuyi (elm fruit paste). Taste pungent. Indicated for evil qi within the five viscera; disperses creeping toxin moving through the skin and joints. Expels the three worms and aids digestion. Also called Wugu; an alternate name is partly illegible in the source text. Grows in river valleys.

Sanggen baipi (mulberry root bark). Taste sweet, cold. Indicated for internal injury, the five taxations and six extremities with emaciation, and flooding with pulse collapse; supplements deficiency and boosts qi. The leaf removes cold-heat and induces sweating. Mulberry ear (fungus growing on the mulberry tree), the black variety, treats women's vaginal discharge with red-and-white fluid, blood disorders, concretions and accumulations with abdominal pain, yin-yang cold-heat, and infertility. The 'five wood ears' (fungi growing on five kinds of trees), also called Ru, boost qi, stave off hunger, lighten the body, and strengthen the will.

Songluo (usnea / tree-moss lichen). Taste bitter, neutral. Indicated for evil qi of anger and rage; stops deficiency sweating, treats wind in the head, and cold swelling pain in women's genitals. Also called Nüluo. Grows in mountain valleys.

Wujia (Acanthopanax). Taste pungent, warm. Indicated for hernia-qi (shan) pain in the heart and abdomen, and abdominal pain; boosts qi and treats lameness. Enables young children to stand and walk early; treats flat welling-sores and erosive sores of the genital region. Also called Chaiqi.

Zhuling (Polyporus umbellatus). Taste sweet, neutral. Indicated for malarial disorders; resolves toxin, gu-toxin and gu-infestation, and inauspicious influences; promotes urination. Taken long-term, lightens the body and delays aging. Also called Jiazhushi (lit. 'boar's dung'). Grows in mountain valleys.
Baiji (white jujube thorn). Taste pungent, cold. Indicated for heart and abdominal pain, and stops pain from welling-abscess with pus and rupture. Also called Jizhen. Grows in river valleys.

Weimao (winged spindle tree, Euonymus alatus). Taste bitter, cold; non-toxic. Indicated for women's flooding with vaginal bleeding, and abdominal fullness with sweating; dispels evil, and kills ghost-toxin and gu-infestation. Also called Guijian (ghost arrow).

Huanghuan. Taste bitter; toxic. Indicated for gu-toxin, ghost-infestation and ghost-possession, and evil qi lodged within the viscera; removes cough with rebellious qi and cold-heat. Also called Lingquan, Dajiu. Grows in mountain valleys.
